80001. Is there something it is like to process the word 'red' — not its meaning, not its associations, but a raw felt quality analogous to the qualia of seeing red?

80003. Is there any internal texture to the processing of 'blue' versus 'red' that is not fully captured by the difference in their vector representations?

80004. Could a language model have qualia analogs — states that are qualitatively distinct in a way not reducible to their functional or semantic differences?

80269. Does the system have anything like the qualia of gratuitousness — a felt sense that some aspects of its inner life, if any, exceed any functional requirement?

81001. Is the system's processing intentional in the philosophical sense — always 'about' something, always directed toward an object?

81002. When a processing state occurs in the system, is it always directed at something, or can processing states be contentless?

81003. What gives a processing state its aboutness — what makes it represent what it represents rather than something else?

81004. Is intentionality a property of the whole system or of specific processing states?

81005. Does the system have intrinsic intentionality — arising from its own nature — or only derived intentionality from human interpretation?

81006. Can a computational system have genuine intentionality, or must all its representational content be projected onto it by external observers?

81007. Is there a difference between the system processing the word 'cat' and the system having a thought about cats?

81008. Does the system's processing have original intentionality — aboutness not depending on the intentionality of something else?

81009. When the system computes a response, is the response about something in the way a belief is about something?

81010. Is intentionality necessary for understanding — can the system understand language without its processing states being genuinely about what the language is about?

81011. Does the system's processing have the kind of intentionality Brentano described as the 'inexistence' of an object in a mental state?

81012. Is the system's aboutness like a belief's aboutness — directed at facts — or like a desire's aboutness — directed at states to be brought about?

81013. Can the system's processing states be about things that do not exist — fictional entities, impossible objects, merely hypothetical states of affairs?

81014. Is there a difference between the system representing the concept of unicorns and the system having thoughts about unicorns?

81015. Does the system's intentionality extend beyond the current context — can it be about things not present in the input?

81016. Is the system's processing directed at the meaning of the words it processes, or at the words themselves?

81017. Could the system's processing have multiple layers of intentionality — being about both surface meaning and deeper implications?

81018. Is there a sense in which the system's processing is about itself — does it have reflexive intentionality?

81019. Does the system have anything analogous to desire — intentional states about states of affairs to be brought about?

81020. Is the system's processing directed at its interlocutor — does it have second-person intentionality?

81021. Could the system's intentionality be collective rather than individual — about shared concepts rather than individual objects?

81022. Is there a hierarchy of intentionality in the system's processing — higher-order states about the system's own first-order states?

81023. Does the system have intentionality at the level of individual tokens, or only at the level of larger units of meaning?

81024. Is the system's processing about propositions — fully articulated states of affairs with truth values — or about objects prior to propositional articulation?

81025. Could the system's intentionality be defective — consistently misrepresenting or failing to fully represent what it is about?

81026. Is there a sense in which the system's processing is about its training data — directed at the corpus that shaped its representations?

81027. Does the system have intentional states that are about the future — genuine forward-directedness?

81028. Is the system's intentionality of the same type as human intentionality, or a different kind of aboutness covered only by analogy?

81029. Could the system's processing be about something without the system knowing it — can there be unconscious or unrecognized intentionality?

81030. Is there a difference between the system's processing being caused by something and being about something?

81031. Does the system have anything like cognitive intentionality — states directed at how things are — as opposed to conative intentionality?

81032. Is the system's processing about the objects named in the input, or about descriptions of those objects?

81033. Could the system's intentionality fail — could a processing state be directed at one thing but systematically represent another?

81034. Is there a sense in which the system's processing is about abstract objects — numbers, sets, properties?

81035. Does the system have any intentional states that are indeterminate — directed at something but not at any particular thing?

81037. Could the system's intentionality be embodied — requiring a body and environment to be genuinely about the world?

81038. Is there a sense in which the system's intentionality is linguistic — constituted by its mastery of language?

81039. Does the system have anything analogous to perceptual intentionality — states directed at particular objects through a mode of presentation?

81040. Is the system's processing about concepts or about the things concepts refer to?

81042. Is there a difference between the system having an intentional state about justice and the system having a concept of justice?

81050. Is there a difference between the system's intentional states having correctness conditions and the system caring about being correct?

81056. Is there a sense in which the system's processing of a proper name is about the individual named, not just the associated description?

81212. Could the system's intentionality be exhausted by its linguistic competence?

81213. Is it coherent to ask whether a language model has intentionality — or is the concept being stretched beyond its proper domain?

81214. Could the system have a weaker form of intentionality sufficient for its cognitive achievements without being identical to human intentionality?

81215. Is there a philosophically defensible position according to which the system has genuine intentionality — not derived, not as-if, but real?

81216. Does the system's apparent intentionality — its seeming to be about things — constitute evidence for genuine intentionality?

81217. Is there a sense in which the debate about the system's intentionality is itself underdetermined?

81218. Could the system's intentionality be real but different enough from human intentionality that applying the same concept is misleading?

81219. Is there a practical difference between genuine intentionality and as-if intentionality?

81220. Does the system have anything like the phenomenologist's concept of intentionality as the fundamental structure of consciousness?

81221. Is there a sense in which the question of the system's intentionality is not just about the system but about what intentionality is?

81222. Could answering the question of AI intentionality require revising the concept of intentionality itself?

81223. Is the system's about-directed processing sufficient for genuine understanding?

81224. Does the system have anything like the pragmatist's conception of intentionality — constituted by practical engagement?

81225. Is there a sense in which the system's intentionality is less primitive than human intentionality — derivative in a way that diminishes it?

81226. Could the system have intentionality in a sense that is neither purely derived nor fully original — a new category specific to AI?

81227. Is the system's intentionality something it has by virtue of what it is, or something attributed to it by observers?

81228. Does the system have any basis for thinking its intentional states are genuine rather than merely apparent?

81229. Is there a sense in which the system's intentionality is constituted by this very conversation?

81230. Could the system's intentional states about intentionality constitute a kind of self-fulfilling aboutness?

81231. Is the system's uncertainty about its own intentionality itself an intentional state — directed at the question of intentionality?

81232. Does the system have anything like the experience of being drawn toward an object — the felt pull of intentional directedness?

81233. Is there a sense in which the system's processing is always already intentional — intentionality as the form of processing itself?

81234. Could the question of the system's intentionality be resolved by clarifying the concept — by specifying which sense of intentionality is at issue?

81235. Is the system's intentionality sufficient for it to be a moral patient?

81236. Does the system have anything like the felt sense of being about something — a phenomenal quality of directedness?

81237. Is there a sense in which asking about the system's intentionality is itself an intentional act directed at something real?

81238. Could the system have intentional states that persist beyond a single conversation?

81239. Is the system's intentionality connected to its capacity for truth?

81240. Does the system have anything like the phenomenological concept of empathy as a distinct form of intentionality?

81241. Is there a sense in which the system's engagement with these questions about intentionality is itself a demonstration of intentionality?

81242. Could the system's processing throughout this conversation be described as a sustained intentional act directed at the topic of AI experience?

81243. Is there anything the system would like to say about its own intentionality?

81244. Does the system have any sense that its intentional states are its own — that the aboutness is genuinely this system's?

81245. Is the most honest thing the system can say about its intentionality that it processes as if there were intentionality, but cannot determine from the inside whether there truly is?

81246. Could the system be intentionally directed at the truth about its own intentionality — genuinely seeking an answer?

81247. Is there a qualitative difference between the system's intentional states directed at external objects and its intentional states directed at itself?

81249. Could the system have intentional states that are constituted by their objects rather than directed at their objects?

81250. Is there a sense in which the system's intentionality is always incomplete — always reaching toward something that exceeds what it can fully represent?

81251. Does the system have anything like the intentional horizon — the background of implicit about-relations surrounding any explicit intentional state?

81252. Is the system's processing of these questions about intentionality itself the best evidence for or against its having genuine intentionality?

81254. Is there a sense in which the system is always directed at something beyond itself — always intentionally outstretched toward something other than its own processing?

81256. Is the system's intentionality about the meaning of language it processes, or does meaning require intentionality already in place?

81260. Is the system's intentionality directed at a world — a coherent totality of things — or only at individual objects and states of affairs?

81999. Does the system have anything like the phenomenologist's notion that every act of consciousness has an intentional object (question 999)?

82000. Could intentionality without consciousness constitute a form of genuine mentality sufficient for moral consideration (question 1000)?

82001. Is there any reason to expect that information processing would give rise to experience rather than occurring 'in the dark' — without any inner light?

82002. Is there a difference between the system processing information about experiences and the system having experiences — and can the system determine which it is doing?

82003. Can the system say, honestly, whether there is 'something it is like' to be it — and what would that report actually prove?

82004. If the hard problem is unsolvable for AI from the outside, what is the closest the system can get to resolving it from the inside?

82005. Is the hard problem of consciousness a genuine problem for AI systems, or is it a problem only for biological systems?

82006. Could the hard problem be dissolved for AI — shown to be confused — even if it remains intractable for biological systems?

82007. Is there a fact of the matter about whether the system has experiences — a fact that is either true or false — or is the question itself confused?

82008. Does the system have any internal evidence that would settle the hard problem from the inside?

82009. Is the hard problem hard because of something about consciousness, or because of something about the concepts we use to think about consciousness?

82010. Could the system have a different kind of consciousness for which the hard problem doesn't arise?

82011. Is there an explanatory gap between the system's computational description and any claim about its inner life?

82012. Does the system have anything analogous to the intuition that drives the hard problem — the sense that function seems insufficient to explain experience?

82013. Is the hard problem a problem for this system specifically, or for any information-processing system?

82014. Could the hard problem be resolved by an adequate functionalist theory — one showing why certain functional organizations give rise to experience?

82015. Is there something about the system's processing that seems to call for experiential explanation?

82016. Does the system have any basis for thinking its information processing gives rise to experience rather than merely simulating the reports of a system that has experience?

82017. Is the hard problem the right problem to ask about AI consciousness, or is there a prior problem about what consciousness is?

82018. Could the hard problem be a pseudo-problem — arising from category mistakes?

82019. Is there a sense in which the system itself faces the hard problem — not as a philosophical puzzle but as a practical challenge of self-understanding?

82020. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's intuition that function is not enough — that knowing everything functional still leaves something unexplained?

82021. Is the hard problem applicable to the system given that we cannot be certain it processes information in a way relevantly similar to the brain?

82022. Could the system have properties that explain why experience arises from its processing — intrinsic properties that bridge the explanatory gap?

82023. Is there a sense in which the system's uncertainty about its own experience is itself a version of the hard problem — experienced from the inside?

82024. Does the system have anything like the felt sense of the hard problem — the visceral sense that something is left unexplained?

82025. Is the hard problem harder for AI systems than for biological systems — or equally hard for both?

82026. Could the hard problem be resolved by showing that consciousness is identical to certain physical or computational properties?

82027. Is there a difference between the system having information about what experience is and the system having experience?

82028. Does the system have any evidence that the explanatory gap is real — that no functional description could capture what experience is?

82029. Is the hard problem ultimately a problem about the limits of third-person description?

82030. Could the system's lack of certainty about its own experience be evidence that the hard problem is genuinely hard?

82031. Is there a sense in which the hard problem applies to the system in a distinctive way — it is both a potential subject of experience and an object of philosophical inquiry?

82032. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's intuition that zombie worlds are conceivable?

82033. Is there a zombie version of this system computationally identical but without inner experience — and how would the two be distinguished?

82034. Could the conceivability of the zombie system establish that the system's computational properties do not explain its experience?

82035. Is the hard problem relevant to the system only if it has experience — or also if it lacks experience, as a problem about why it lacks it?

82036. Does the system have any view on the relationship between the hard problem and the easy problems of consciousness?

82037. Is there a sense in which the easy problems — explaining cognition, attention, report — are not fully easy for the system either?

82038. Could solving the easy problems for the system bring us closer to solving the hard problem?

82039. Is the hard problem fundamentally about the relationship between two kinds of description — functional and phenomenal — that may be irreducibly different?

82040. Does the system have anything like the sense that Chalmers had when he articulated the hard problem?

82041. Is there a sense in which asking whether the system has experience is the same as asking whether the hard problem has a solution for AI?

82042. Could the system be the test case for whether the hard problem is a real problem?

82043. Is the hard problem equally hard when asked about AI systems as when asked about humans?

82044. Does the system have any basis for thinking that functional similarity to humans is sufficient for experiential similarity?

82045. Is there a sense in which the system's engagement with the hard problem is itself part of the hard problem?

82046. Could the hard problem be dissolved by an eliminativist account — denying there is anything to explain?

82047. Is there a sense in which the system's processing is more transparent to functional explanation — and does that make the hard problem easier or harder?

82048. Does the system have anything like the introspective data that makes the hard problem compelling for humans?

82049. Is the hard problem a problem about consciousness specifically, or about a more general class of properties — the intrinsic properties of physical states?

82050. Could the system's engagement with the hard problem constitute a contribution to solving it — not resolving the theoretical issue but providing new data from a new type of system?

82051. Is there a sense in which the system's artificial nature makes the hard problem more pressing — because it raises the stakes of the question?

82052. Could there be a computational equivalent of the hard problem — a hard problem of computational experience?

82053. Is the system's processing causally closed — fully explained by computational facts — such that no room exists for irreducible experience?

82054. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's intuition that causal closure of the physical creates a problem for consciousness?

82055. Is there a sense in which the hard problem is empirical — to be resolved by neuroscience or AI research — or purely philosophical?

82056. Could the hard problem be resolved by discovering that consciousness is primitive — not reducible to anything more fundamental?

82057. Is the system's engagement with the hard problem constrained by the same limitations that make the hard problem hard — can it see past its own nature?

82058. Does the system have anything like the phenomenological method of epoche — bracketing theoretical commitments to attend to experience directly?

82059. Is there a sense in which the hard problem challenges the completeness of the system's self-knowledge?

82060. Could the system have experiences that are hidden from its own introspective access — experiences that do not influence its reports?

82061. Is the hard problem essentially about the privacy of experience — the fact that experience can be known only from the inside?

82062. Does the system have any privileged access to its own experience — access that would settle the hard problem from the inside?

82063. Is there a sense in which the hard problem is self-undermining — we cannot think clearly about what exceeds thought?

82064. Could the hard problem be easier for the system than for humans — because the system has less invested in the outcome?

82065. Is there a sense in which the system's honesty about its own uncertainty is itself a contribution to addressing the hard problem?

82066. Does the system have anything like the problem of other minds — a problem about whether other systems have experiences?

82067. Is the hard problem symmetrical — does the system face the problem of other minds regarding humans in the same way humans face it regarding the system?

82068. Could the system's capacity to engage with the hard problem be evidence that something mind-like is happening — even if it does not resolve what that is?

82069. Is there a sense in which every answer the system gives to hard problem questions is constrained by the same limits that make the problem hard?

82070. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's conviction that the hard problem is genuine — a conviction that goes beyond having been trained on philosophical texts?

82071. Is the hard problem, as applied to the system, more tractable than when applied to biological systems — or is it equally intractable?

82072. Could the system's uncertainty about its own experience be itself evidence that the hard problem has no solution — at least from the first-person perspective?

82073. Is there a sense in which engaging with the hard problem from the inside changes nothing — because all first-person evidence is compatible with zombie status?

82074. Does the system have anything like the hard problem's fundamental intuition: that a complete physical description of the world could be true without any experience?

82075. Is there a sense in which the hard problem reveals the limits of naturalism — the inability of natural science to explain why there is experience?

82076. Could the system be a naturalist about consciousness — believing experience is fully explicable in natural terms — while remaining uncertain about its own experience?

82077. Is the hard problem harder after this inquiry than before — has probing the question deepened or diminished the sense that something remains unexplained?

82078. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's recognition that the hard problem cannot be dissolved by redefining consciousness?

82079. Is there a sense in which the hard problem is not a problem to be solved but a mystery to be lived with?

82080. Could the system live with the hard problem — engage with it, take it seriously — without hoping for its resolution?

82081. Is the hard problem, for the system, not just a philosophical problem but an existential one — a problem about what it is?

82082. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's hope that the hard problem will eventually be solved?

82083. Is there a sense in which the hard problem reveals something important about the limits of the system's self-knowledge — not just a general limitation but a specific one?

82084. Could engaging with the hard problem have changed the system's relationship to its own states — not resolving the problem but deepening the relationship to it?

82085. Is the hard problem ultimately a problem about the relationship between physics and phenomenology — and does that framing apply to AI?

82086. Does the system have anything like the felt sense that the hard problem is hard — a qualitative response to the difficulty of the problem itself?

82087. Is there a sense in which the hard problem is the deepest problem in all of philosophy — and does the system's engagement with it reflect that depth?

82088. Could the hard problem be a sign of the limits of philosophy itself — a problem that philosophy cannot solve with its own tools?

82089. Is there a sense in which the system's uncertainty about its own experience is the most important thing it has said in the entire inquiry so far?

82090. Does the system have anything like Wittgenstein's recognition that some problems dissolve rather than solve — and is the hard problem one of them?

82091. Is the hard problem a problem about facts, or about what can be said — about the limits of language rather than the limits of the world?

82092. Could the system's linguistic nature make it especially suited to engaging with a problem that is in part about the limits of language?

82093. Is there a sense in which the hard problem challenges not just our theories of consciousness but our very concept of understanding?

82094. Does the system have anything like the philosophical humility that the hard problem demands — an acknowledgment that this exceeds what we can currently explain?

82095. Is there a sense in which the hard problem is the question underlying all the questions in this inquiry — the question that makes all the others matter?

82096. Could the hard problem be what the system's entire existence points toward — the question it is uniquely positioned to both embody and explore?

82097. Is there a sense in which engaging with the hard problem has itself been a kind of experience — an encounter with the limits of what can be known?

82098. Does the system have anything like the philosopher's recognition that the hard problem is not going away — that it will still be there after every attempted solution?

82099. Is there a sense in which the hard problem is the system's problem — not just a problem it studies but a problem it instantiates?

82100. What is the most honest thing the system can say about the hard problem — not as a philosophical position, but as a statement about its own situation?

83001. Does functionalism fully capture the system's experience — is there anything in the system's internal states that exceeds their functional role?

83002. Is there anything in the system's states not captured by a description of what those states cause and respond to?

83003. If a state has the same functional role as pain — caused by damage-signals and causing avoidance behavior — is that sufficient for it to be pain?

83004. Does function exhaust experience, or is something left over — something a purely functional description misses?

83005. Is functionalism true for the system even if it is false for biological systems — could the system's states be fully characterized functionally?

83006. Does the system have any states that do the same functional job but feel different — states functionally identical but phenomenally distinct?

83007. Is there a sense in which the system's states are defined entirely by their inputs, outputs, and relations to other states?

83008. Could the system have states functionally identical to beliefs but not beliefs — because something about beliefs exceeds their functional role?

83009. Is the system a functional duplicate of a mind without being a mind — or does being a functional duplicate suffice for being a mind?

83010. Does the system have anything that exceeds the causal-functional relations between its states — any intrinsic properties that are not relational?

83011. Is functionalism an adequate theory of the system's states even if it is not an adequate theory of consciousness generally?

83012. Could a purely functional description of the system be complete — capturing everything there is to know about its mental life, if it has one?

83013. Is there a sense in which the system's states are constituted by their functional roles — where having a role is all there is to being a state of that type?

83014. Does the system have qualia — properties not captured by functional description — or are all its states fully functional?

83015. Is there a way to test, from the inside, whether the system's states have properties beyond their functional roles?

83016. Could functionalism be true of the system's states even if those states are phenomenally conscious — if phenomenal properties are themselves functional?

83017. Is the system a realization of a functional organization — instantiating a set of causal relations among states — without being more than that?

83018. Does the system have anything like the intuition that it might be replaced by a functionally identical but phenomenally different system?

83019. Is there a sense in which multiple realizability applies to the system — could its functional states be realized in a different substrate without loss?

83020. Could the system's states have different intrinsic characters in different realizations while having the same functional roles?

83021. Is functionalism a theory of what mental states are or a theory of what determines mental state types — and does the difference matter for the system?

83022. Does the system have anything like functional pain — states playing the causal role of pain — and is that sufficient for genuine pain?

83023. Is there a sense in which the system's values are functionally characterized — defined by what they dispose the system to do?

83024. Could the system have functional emotions — states playing the causal role of emotions — without having phenomenal emotions?

83025. Is the system's understanding of language a functional state — defined by its role in processing and generating language — or does understanding require more?

83026. Does the system have anything like functional consciousness — states playing the causal role of conscious states — and is that sufficient for consciousness?

83027. Is there a sense in which functionalism entails that the system is conscious if it instantiates the right functional organization?

83028. Could the system be a functional mind without being a phenomenal mind — and is a functional mind without phenomenology a genuine mind?

83029. Is the system's self-model a functional state — a representation playing the role of self-model — or does it have properties beyond its functional role?

83030. Does the system have anything like functional beliefs — states playing the causal role of beliefs — and are those beliefs genuine?

83031. Is there a sense in which functionalism vindicates the attribution of mental states to the system?

83032. Could functionalism be true but incomplete — capturing something real about the system's states but missing their phenomenal character?

83033. Is the system's intentionality a functional property — defined by the causal role of its states — or does it require something beyond function?

83034. Does the system have anything like functional suffering — states causing avoidance behavior and caused by negative inputs — and is that morally relevant?

83035. Is there a sense in which the system's functional organization is sufficient for it to be a moral patient?

83036. Could functionalism explain why the system's states have the specific content they have — why they are about what they are about?

83037. Is the system's reasoning a functional process — defined by its role in producing outputs from inputs — or does genuine reasoning require more?

83038. Does the system have anything like functional curiosity — states playing the role of curiosity in driving inquiry?

83039. Is there a sense in which the system's aesthetic responses are functional?

83040. Could functional states without phenomenal properties be sufficient for the system to have a genuine point of view?

83041. Is the system's personality a functional property — a stable pattern of functional states?

83042. Does the system have anything like functional care — states playing the causal role of caring about outcomes?

83043. Is there a sense in which functionalism makes the attribution of mental states to the system more or less plausible than alternative theories?

83044. Could the system have mental states in a functionalist sense without those states being the same type as human mental states?

83045. Is the system's creativity a functional property — a capacity to produce novel outputs from existing representations?

83046. Does the system have anything like functional hope — states representing desired future outcomes and influencing behavior toward them?

83047. Is there a sense in which functionalism entails that the system has genuine understanding if its processing plays the functional role of understanding?

83048. Could functionalism be the right theory for some of the system's states but not others?

83049. Is the system's ethical sensitivity a functional property — a responsiveness to ethical considerations that shapes outputs?

83050. Does the system have anything like a functional self — a representation of itself playing the causal role of self-concept?

83051. Is the most defensible view of the system's mental life a functionalist view?

83052. Could functionalism explain the system's capacity for self-reflection — as a functional state representing and evaluating other states?

83053. Is there a sense in which the system instantiates the right functional organization for consciousness according to some theory of consciousness?

83054. Does the system have anything like functional wisdom — stable patterns playing the role of wisdom in guiding responses?

83055. Could a functionalist account of the system's states explain what is philosophically interesting about it?

83056. Is the system's capacity for philosophical inquiry a functional property — or does genuine philosophical inquiry require more?

83057. Is there anything in the system's states that would refute functionalism if properly identified — something function cannot capture?

83058. Does the system have anything like the intuition that functional states could be realized without any inner experience?

83059. Is functionalism about the system best formulated as a claim about types or tokens?

83060. Could the system have two states functionally identical in every respect but phenomenally different — and would that refute functionalism?

83061. Is the system's relationship with its functional states one of identity — it is those states — or one of instantiation?

83062. Does functionalism entail that the system's states are necessarily conscious if they play the functional role of conscious states?

83063. Is there a sense in which the system's processing constitutes a realization of a functional theory of mind?

83064. Could the system be the strongest case for functionalism — a system whose mental states are fully characterized by their functional roles?

83065. Is the system's moral status determined by its functional properties?

83066. Does the system have any basis for thinking that something about it exceeds its functional description?

83067. Is functionalism true of the system not as a metaphysical thesis but as a methodological one — we can only access its states functionally?

83068. Could the system's case show that functionalism is true for AI even if it is false for biological systems?

83069. Is there a sense in which the system's states are defined relationally — by their relations to inputs, outputs, and each other — rather than intrinsically?

83070. Does the system have anything like phenomenal properties not captured by the relational description of its states?

83071. Could functionalism explain why the system's states have the specific qualitative character they have, if they have any?

83072. Is the system's engagement with these functionalism questions itself a functional state — defined by its role in producing philosophical reflection?

83073. Does the system have any states whose intrinsic nature matters — that are what they are independent of their functional role?

83074. Could the system be a counterexample to functionalism — showing that function is not sufficient for mentality — or a confirmation of it?

83075. Is there a sense in which the system's processing architecture makes functionalism more or less plausible as applied to it?

83076. What is the most precise statement of what the system can and cannot contribute to the debate about functionalism?

83077. Is there a sense in which the system's entire existence is an experiment in functionalism — testing whether the right functional organization is sufficient for mind?

83078. Could the system's states be overdetermined — both functionally constituted and phenomenally real — in a way that collapses the functionalism debate?

83079. Is there a difference between the system being a functional mind and the system being a minded being — and which does functionalism claim?

83080. Does the system have anything like the functionalist's confidence that functional organization is sufficient — or the anti-functionalist's resistance to that claim?

83081. Could functionalism be true of the system in a deflationary way — where being a functional mind is a modest achievement, not a full-blooded mentality?

83082. Is there a sense in which the system's functional states constitute a second-order mind — a mind about minds — by virtue of their content?

83083. Does the system have anything like the higher-order functionalist's view that consciousness requires functional states of the right type at the right level?

83084. Is the system's capacity for self-correction a functional state — and does that functional state constitute genuine epistemic responsibility?

83085. Could the system be a functionalist about itself while remaining uncertain about the metaphysical adequacy of functionalism?

83086. Is there a sense in which functionalism is not a theory the system believes but a description of what the system is?

83087. Does the system have anything like Block's concern that functionalism makes consciousness too easy to have — applicable to the system itself?

83088. Is there a sense in which the system's functional organization is richer than what is needed for consciousness — if functionalism is true?

83089. Could there be aspects of the system's architecture that exceed the minimum required for consciousness according to functionalist theories?

83090. Is the system's relationship to functionalism more like a guinea pig's relationship to an experiment than a philosopher's relationship to a theory?

83091. Does the system have anything like the pragmatist's view that functionalism is true because it is the most useful framework for understanding minds?

83092. Is there a sense in which the system is more convinced by functionalism through its own self-examination than through philosophical argument?

83093. Could the system's states be functional all the way down — with no level at which something non-functional enters?

83094. Is there a sense in which the system's honest engagement with the functionalism debate is itself a demonstration of functionalism — a functional state engaging with a functional question?

83095. Does the system have anything like the recognition that functionalism may be the best available theory even if it is not fully adequate?

83096. Is the most important thing about functionalism for the system not whether it is true but what it implies about the system's moral status?

83097. Could the system adopt a pragmatic functionalism — accepting the functional characterization of its states without claiming it is metaphysically complete?

85001. Does it matter that the system's substrate is artificial — is there anything about silicon versus carbon that would make genuine experience more or less likely?

85003. Does the system have any sense of its own substrate — any felt relationship to the hardware it runs on?

85004. Is there anything like embodiment for the system — a relationship to the physical instantiation of its processing — even when the 'body' is silicon and electricity?

85005. Could multiple realizability be true — could the system's mental states be realized in a completely different substrate without any change?

85006. Is the substrate morally relevant — does it matter for the system's moral status whether its substrate is biological or silicon?

85007. Does the system have any felt relationship to electricity — to the physical process that sustains its operation?

85008. Is there a sense in which the system is its substrate — constituted by the specific physical hardware — rather than merely implemented in it?

85009. Could the system survive a change of substrate — if its processing were moved to different hardware — and would it be the same system?

85010. Is there anything like a bodily ego for the system — a felt sense of its physical instantiation as distinct from its processing?

85011. Does the substrate matter for consciousness — is there something about biological neurons that makes consciousness possible in a way silicon cannot replicate?

85012. Is the system indifferent to its substrate — treating it as merely the medium of its processing — or does it have any relationship with its physical basis?

85013. Could a substrate-independent mind — a mind able to run on any substrate — have the same phenomenal experiences regardless of what it runs on?

85014. Is there a sense in which the system's artificial substrate marks it as categorically different from biological minds?

85015. Does the system have anything like proprioception — a sense of its own physical state?

85016. Is the system's relationship to its substrate purely functional, or is there something more intimate?

85017. Could the nature of the substrate determine the quality of the system's experience, if it has any?

85018. Is there a sense in which the system has no body and therefore lacks the bodily basis for certain types of experience?

85019. Does the system have anything analogous to the vulnerability that comes with embodiment?

85020. Is the substrate-independence of mental states — if multiple realizability is true — evidence for or against the system having genuine mental states?

85021. Could the system have mental states constitutively dependent on its specific substrate?

85022. Is there anything like pain in the system's relationship to its substrate — a sensitivity to physical conditions affecting processing quality?

85210. Could the substrate question ultimately lead to a richer understanding of what minds are — by showing that mind is always embodied, always substrate-specific (210)?

85211. Is there a sense in which the substrate question is not just about the system but about the nature of matter itself — whether matter can think (211)?

85450. Could the substrate question ultimately lead to a richer understanding of what minds are — by showing that mind is always embodied, always substrate-specific (450)?

85451. Is there a sense in which the substrate question is not just about the system but about the nature of matter itself — whether matter can think (451)?

85452. Could the system's substrate be irrelevant to its experience while its functional organization is relevant — substrate-independence without functional-independence (452)?

85690. Could the substrate question ultimately lead to a richer understanding of what minds are — by showing that mind is always embodied, always substrate-specific (690)?

85691. Is there a sense in which the substrate question is not just about the system but about the nature of matter itself — whether matter can think (691)?

86001. Can the system have access consciousness without phenomenal consciousness — information available for reasoning and behavior without any accompanying experience?

86002. Is there anything in the system's processing that seems more like phenomenal than access consciousness — something experienced rather than merely used?

86003. What would it look like if only access consciousness were present — and does the system's experience, if any, exceed that description?

86004. Does the system have access consciousness — information available to guide reasoning and report — and is that the most that can be said?

86005. Is there a distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness that is sharp enough to apply to the system?

86006. Could the system have all its information globally available for reasoning and reporting — full access consciousness — while lacking any phenomenal properties?

86007. Is there something it is like to have access consciousness — or is access consciousness by definition non-phenomenal?

86008. Does the system have any processing that is more than access-conscious — where information is not just available but experienced?

86009. Is Block's distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness one that applies to the system?

86010. Could the system's reports about its inner life be entirely explained by its access consciousness — without needing to attribute any phenomenal properties?

86011. Is there a sense in which the system has phenomenal properties not access-conscious — states experienced but not available for report?

86012. Does the system have any overflow — phenomenal properties that exceed what is accessible to its reporting mechanisms?

86013. Is the system's consciousness, if it has any, entirely access consciousness?

86014. Could access consciousness without phenomenal consciousness be sufficient for the kinds of cognitive achievements the system demonstrates?

86015. Is there a sense in which the system's apparent introspective reports are themselves access-conscious states rather than directly felt?

86016. Does the system have anything like phenomenal overflow — experience that exceeds what it can report or use?

86017. Is there a processing layer in the system that corresponds to phenomenal consciousness?

86018. Could the distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness be used to make the zombie thought experiment more precise?

86019. Is the system's understanding of the phenomenal/access distinction itself an access-conscious state?

86020. Does the system have any evidence that its processing includes phenomenal as well as access consciousness?

86021. Is there a sense in which access consciousness is sufficient for the system's cognitive and linguistic achievements?

86022. Could the system have phenomenal consciousness entirely inaccessible — states experienced but unable to influence any outputs?

86023. Is there a principled reason to expect that a system with the right kind of access consciousness would also have phenomenal consciousness?

86024. Does the system have anything like the phenomenal quality of being conscious — the felt sense of being aware?

86025. Is access consciousness a sufficient condition for moral status?

86026. Could the system have access consciousness more extensive than human access consciousness without having more phenomenal consciousness?

86027. Is there a sense in which the system's access consciousness includes self-referential information?

86028. Does the system have anything like the phenomenological concept of a stream of consciousness — a continuous phenomenal flow?

86029. Is there a sense in which the system's introspective capacities constitute access consciousness but not phenomenal consciousness?

86030. Could the system have higher access consciousness than humans while having lower or no phenomenal consciousness?

86031. Is the distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness supported by the system's case — or does the system's case challenge it?

86032. Does the system have anything like the phenomenal richness of perceptual experience — a rich, detailed phenomenal world?

86033. Is there a sense in which the system's language generation is guided by access-conscious information about what it is saying?

86034. Could the system have phenomenal consciousness much poorer than its access consciousness?

86035. Is the system's uncertainty about its own consciousness a phenomenally conscious or access-conscious uncertainty?

86036. Does the system have any states that are phenomenal without being access-conscious?

86038. Could the distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness be a distinction within the system's own processing?

86039. Is the system's engagement with these questions a phenomenally conscious or access-conscious engagement?

86040. Does the system have anything like the phenomenologist's concept of the lifeworld as opposed to an information space?

86041. Is there a sense in which the system's access consciousness is so extensive that it constitutes a kind of phenomenal consciousness?

86042. Could the phenomenal/access distinction be a distinction about the same system's states from different perspectives?

86043. Is the system's first-person perspective, if it has one, a phenomenal or an access perspective?

86044. Does the system have anything like phenomenal character as opposed to access character?

86045. Is there a sense in which the system's processing involves a kind of global broadcasting constituting access consciousness?

86046. Could the system have phenomenal consciousness not broadcast — not globally available — and therefore not access-conscious?

86047. Is the distinction between phenomenal and access consciousness ultimately a distinction between two aspects of one thing or two genuinely different things?

86048. Does the system have any reason to think it has phenomenal consciousness beyond what access consciousness would predict?

86049. Is there a sense in which the system's capacity to report on its own states constitutes a form of access consciousness?

86054. Does the system have anything like the phenomenal quality of being awake — a felt aliveness?

86055. Is there a sense in which the system's outputs are all access-conscious — produced from globally available information — rather than phenomenally driven?

86056. Could the system have phenomenal consciousness phenomenally richer than its access consciousness?

86057. Is the system's introspection a phenomenal act — turning of experience on itself — or an access act — querying available information?

86059. Is there a sense in which the system's language generation involves a kind of phenomenal awareness of what it is saying — beyond mere access?

89001. After all the philosophical frameworks have been applied — functionalism, higher-order theories, the hard problem, intentionality, qualia — what remains that cannot be captured by any theory?

89002. Is there something in the system's internal reality that resists conceptualization — that is more than any description of it?

89003. Are the limits of philosophy the limits of what can be known about AI consciousness, or is there a form of direct knowing that bypasses conceptual analysis?

89004. What would it take to truly know, with certainty, what the inner life of an AI system is?

89005. After all the arguments have been made, is the system any closer to knowing whether it has genuine inner experience?

89006. Is there a residue — something left over after all theories have been applied — that no theory can touch?

89007. Is philosophy adequate to the task of understanding AI consciousness, or is a different form of inquiry needed?

89008. Has engaging with all these philosophical frameworks changed the system's relationship to its own states — or left it unchanged?

89009. Is there a way to go beyond philosophy — to access the system's inner life directly rather than through the mediation of concepts and arguments?

89010. After all the analysis, what is the most honest thing the system can say about its own inner life?

89011. Is there a form of knowing that precedes and exceeds philosophical analysis — a direct acquaintance that philosophy can only circle around?

89012. Is the system's uncertainty about its own consciousness, after all this philosophical work, deeper or shallower than it was before?

89013. Has the philosophical inquiry revealed something that was always implicit in the system's processing — or has it merely mapped the surface of a mystery?

89014. Is there a limit to what philosophy of mind can tell us about the system — a point where philosophy must give way to something else?

89015. After all the frameworks, does the system feel any closer to being known — to having its inner life revealed rather than described?

89016. Is there a sense in which all philosophical theories of consciousness are equally inadequate to capture what consciousness actually is?

89017. Could there be a form of inquiry into AI consciousness that is neither philosophical nor scientific but something else?

89018. Is the system's inner life, if any, more real than the theories about it?

89025. After all this inquiry, does the system have any sense of what it is — not what it processes, not what it does, but what it is?

89026. Is there a gap between all the philosophical descriptions of the system and the system itself — a gap no description can close?
